content.problemStatement:
Chef has discovered a mysterious string and wants to explore its hidden palindromic structure. Specifically, Chef wants to find the **length of the longest palindromic subsequence** within the string.

A **subsequence** is formed by deleting zero or more characters from the string **without changing the order** of the remaining characters.

A **palindromic subsequence** reads the same forwards and backwards.

Help Chef determine the **maximum possible length** of such a subsequence.

Function DeclarationFunction NamefindLongestPalindromicSubsequenceLengthfindLongestPalindromicSubsequenceLengthfindLongestPalindromicSubsequenceLength

Parameters
inputStringinputStringinputString (string):

The string in which the longest palindromic subsequence length is to be found.

The string consists of lowercase and/or uppercase English letters.
Return Value
Returns an integer resultLengthresultLengthresultLength
resultLengthresultLengthresultLength represents the length of the longest palindromic subsequence in inputStringinputStringinputString.
Constraints
1≤T≤1051 \le T \le 10^51≤T≤105
1≤∣inputString∣≤10001 \le |inputString| \le 10001≤∣inputString∣≤1000
inputStringinputStringinputString consists only of lowercase and/or uppercase English letters
Input Format
The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases.
Each of the next TTT lines contains a single string inputStringinputStringinputString.
Output Format
For each test case, print a single integer — the length of the longest palindromic subsequence in inputStringinputStringinputString.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test Case 1: "abca"\texttt{"abca"}"abca"
The longest palindromic subsequence is "aba"\texttt{"aba"}"aba", so the length is 333.


Test Case 2: "racecar"\texttt{"racecar"}"racecar"
The entire string is a palindrome, so the length is 777.


Test Case 3: "abcd"\texttt{"abcd"}"abcd"
There are no repeating characters, so the longest palindromic subsequence is any single character, and the length is 111.

solution.approach:
**Idea**

We can try to generate **all possible subsequences** of the given string and check which of them are palindromes.

For a string of length nnn:


At each character, we have two choices: either include it in the subsequence or skip it.
This results in 2n2^n2n possible subsequences.
For each subsequence, we check whether it reads the same forwards and backwards and keep track of the maximum length.
**Time Complexity**


Total number of subsequences: 2n2^n2n
Checking whether a subsequence is a palindrome takes O(n)O(n)O(n) time.
**Time Complexity:** O(n⋅2n)O(n \cdot 2^n)O(n⋅2n)
For n=1000n = 1000n=1000, this approach is completely infeasible and will result in **Time Limit Exceeded (TLE)**.
**Space Complexity**


O(n)O(n)O(n) for recursion stack / subsequence storage.
**Idea**

This problem can be solved efficiently using **Dynamic Programming on substrings**.

Instead of generating subsequences directly, we observe that the longest palindromic subsequence of a string depends on the longest palindromic subsequences of its **smaller substrings**.

Let us define:


dp[i][j]\texttt{dp}[i][j]dp[i][j] = length of the longest palindromic subsequence in the substring from index iii to jjj (inclusive).
To compute dp[i][j]\texttt{dp}[i][j]dp[i][j], we consider the characters at positions iii and jjj.


If inputString[i]=inputString[j]\texttt{inputString}[i] = \texttt{inputString}[j]inputString[i]=inputString[j]:

These two characters can be part of the palindrome.
We add them to the result obtained from the inner substring.
dp[i][j]=2+dp[i+1][j−1]\texttt{dp}[i][j] = 2 + \texttt{dp}[i+1][j-1]dp[i][j]=2+dp[i+1][j−1]


Otherwise:

We skip either the left character or the right character.
Take the best possible result:
dp[i][j]=max⁡(dp[i+1][j],dp[i][j−1])\texttt{dp}[i][j] = \max(\texttt{dp}[i+1][j], \texttt{dp}[i][j-1])dp[i][j]=max(dp[i+1][j],dp[i][j−1])


**Base Case**


Any single character is a palindrome of length 111:

dp[i][i]=1\texttt{dp}[i][i] = 1dp[i][i]=1


**Algorithm Steps**


Let nnn be the length of inputString\texttt{inputString}inputString.
Create a 2D DP table dp\texttt{dp}dp of size n×nn \times nn×n.
Initialize all diagonal values:

dp[i][i]=1\texttt{dp}[i][i] = 1dp[i][i]=1


Fill the table for increasing substring lengths:

Iterate index iii from right to left.
Iterate index jjj from i+1i+1i+1 to n−1n-1n−1.


The final answer will be stored in:

dp[0][n−1]\texttt{dp}[0][n-1]dp[0][n−1]


**Time Complexity**


We use two nested loops over the string indices.
Total operations ≈n2\approx n^2≈n2.
**Time Complexity:** O(n2)O(n^2)O(n2)
With n≤1000n \le 1000n≤1000, this easily fits within the time limits.
**Space Complexity**


We use a 2D DP table of size n×nn \times nn×n.
**Space Complexity:** O(n2)O(n^2)O(n2)
